title: Why Won't You Apologize? Healing Big Betrayals and Everyday Hurts
author: Harriet Lerner

chapters.title: Why do some people never apologize?
chapters.text:
Some people easily apologize when they have to. They can even feel connected to the things that were not specifically their fault. For example, "I'm sorry you had an argument with your parents" or "I'm sorry they missed dinner because of the traffic jam."

In contrast to such empathetic people, there are those for whom an apology is similar to a curse word. Therefore, they never use them.

Once, Harriet Lerner was due to give a lecture, and the organization published an outdated photo of her. Despite her comments and the new photo she sent, the first picture appeared in all the printed materials. Instead of an apology, the organization representative advised her to take it easier on herself regarding her appearance.

People who don't receive the expected forgiveness after a formal apology often feel like victims.

What communication approaches can improve the global problem of apologizing? It's time to understand the responsibilities of conflicts and find the right words to explain your position and share your feelings.

chapters.title: The wrong ways to say "I'm sorry"
chapters.text:
In a conflict situation, it's vital to remember not only the need to hear each other and choose the right words but also the critical importance of understanding how not to act. So, what do bad apologies sound like?

• The one that includes a "but"
A denial nullifies regret for what was done and focuses on justifying the actions of the person who offended you. If they want to explain their point this way, they must first be willing to take responsibility for what they have done.

• "I'm sorry that it hurt you"
This is another red flag in an apology and an attempt to make the offender's fault the victim's problem. Let's return to the situation with Lerner's lecture photo. The event manager ignored several requests to change the visual and tried to justify his inattention by being very busy. When that didn't work, he manipulated and "apologized" for Lerner's sensitivity about her appearance and age.

• Anger in the absence of forgiveness
Some people expect to receive an amnesty for their actions immediately after they say the cliché "I'm sorry." Imagine the shock when they don't get one! Instead of being the perpetrators of the conflict, they try to turn themselves into the victims. What they should remember in such situations is that every apology should not only be conscious but also give the other person time and space to experience what happened and forgive them.

• "Look what you made me do"
Lerner remembers when her son didn't want to return a toy to another child, who began banging his head on the floor. The child's mother forced her son to apologize for that injury. However, we cannot take responsibility for each other's actions.

• Obsessive apology
This situation is usually a problem for people who cannot accept rejection. When someone doesn't need or want to hear an apology, others should respect that decision. Constantly explaining will not solve the problem.

"No" is a complete sentence. It should be enough to stop any interactions you don't like.

chapters.title: Conflict + criticism = goodbye forever?
chapters.text:
Evolution has given humans a very important tendency to self-defense. But the nuance is that it appears in both life-threatening situations and everyday family quarrels. When we begin to blame another person for mistakes made, the other party prepares explanations and accusations in response.

An apology is more difficult to offer when we do have something to apologize for and we regret our earlier behavior. ~ Harriet Lerner, PhD

It is most difficult to apologize when we hear criticism addressed to us. But these are moments that are decisive for the future of the relationship. What should we do to avoid separation and move to a new qualitative level of communication?

We apologize sincerely when the bond matters more than the issue.

In relationships with partners, friends, or family members, the most important thing to remember is that it is never too late to change behavior patterns. Even if talking about experiences and emotions was considered a sign of weakness in their families, a couple can create a new tradition by mutual consent. Evening conversations about what is on their minds and hearts will help change the focus from "you" to "me" and reduce the number of accusations, such as "You make me feel bad."

But even under such conditions, no one can guarantee that one of these conversations won't lead to a quarrel in which one partner will come up with 117 points of what is wrong with the other. The other, of course, won't be able to remain silent either and will add another 200 points to the list.

The next few minutes require deep breathing and mutual listening, with a sincere desire to understand each other rather than adding a few more problems. The next step is to try to reconnect without criticism, accusations, or demands for instant feedback. Only when you feel that your partner reciprocates should you offer an apology.

This process may take longer and require additional effort, as you may not be ready to process and accept what you heard immediately. However, both of you must remember that an open heart, questions, and a desire to understand each other will help you reconnect.

chapters.title: A view from another culture
chapters.text:
For a while, Lerner's nephew had a relationship with a woman from Korea. One big difference between their cultures struck him: she never apologized for anything — not to him, her parents, or her friends. Later, he realized that in her mind, there was no point in being sorry for the people closest to her. After all, she believed they would always love her and automatically forgive her for any slight misunderstanding.

We can view all relationships as a clash of two cultures, even if partners or friends are citizens of the same country by birth and grew up in the same neighborhood. After all, people differ in their thinking styles, values, and attitudes to conflicts.

For example, for perfectionists, the need to apologize for something can indicate their imperfection or incompleteness. Admitting it carries the risk of losing their status and the respect of others — even if this fear exists only in their heads and doesn't affect the attitude of their acquaintances or colleagues.

For non-apologizers, making and accepting apologies can seem like a manipulation.

Guilt or shame can also make it difficult to apologize. The former is usually meaningful and justified because someone has done something contrary to their beliefs and needs an apology.

However, shame can be a traumatic experience that makes people feel that what they do and who they are is not enough. In apologies, this has two distinct consequences: people constantly apologize for things they didn't cause, or they blame others and don't admit their mistakes.

Did you know? Gender stereotypes from childhood negatively affect the ability to apologize. For example, a common stereotype for boys is "Be a man!" This applies to the prohibition of crying or appearing weak and an inability to admit mistakes and apologize for them. As for girls, the sexist "Be a lady!" forces them to accept almost all stereotypical apologies not to seem rude and cold.

chapters.title: Between forgiving and forgetting
chapters.text:
Let's shift from apologizing to the other person properly to deciding whether to forgive the wrongdoer.

An unhealthy view of the complexity of forgiveness forms through social media. Thousands of pages with pseudo-philosophical quotes assure us that forgiveness frees us from the burden and indicates inner strength and greatness.

Unfortunately, some people have been treated in such a way that they can neither forgive nor forget what they experienced. Manipulative advice like "You should forgive, and it will become easier for you" can further traumatize them. The only two things they may need are an understanding of their thoughts and the return of their peace.

The desire to forgive an offender and to free yourself from anger or pain are two different goals.

The practice of letting go can help solve this challenge. It doesn't involve forgiveness but acknowledging what happened and being reluctant to hold onto the hurt forever.

Letting go of a big fight or betrayal occurs when victims realize that the pain of the past is preventing them from fully experiencing the good things in the present. Also, by seeking professional help if necessary, they can protect their mental health and regain control of their lives without having to forgive their abuser.

Additionally, under challenging circumstances, people can feel more than one specific emotion but a mix of them.

One day, Lerner's son asked her a theoretical question: Would she continue to communicate with him if he had seriously harmed their family members and gone to prison?

You might think this question is not the most joyful for a young child. However, Lerner used the conversation as an opportunity to talk about the complexity of feelings. Answering his additional questions, she shared that if he committed a crime, she wouldn't forgive him and, at certain moments, she might hate him. But at the same time, she would continue to love him, communicate with him, and visit him in prison.

Thoughts about forgiveness after conflict or traumatic experiences are only the tip of the iceberg. It is worth tracking all the emotions they cause and allowing yourself to experience them. Then, when you are ready, you can start to let them go.

chapters.title: How can you let go?
chapters.text:
Some psychological principles are much easier to describe than to put into practice. So, how do you free yourself from endless memories of your experiences if you can't forgive or forget the offender?

You can start by distinguishing between categories of people and the level of influence their actions and words have on you. For example, how important is it to you if a colleague from a neighboring department remarks on your appearance? Remember that some comments and reactions can be provoked by their mood changes or their private problems and should not affect you in any way.

Don't take things quite so personally; unhappiness or insecurity can make people say stupid things. ~ Harriet Lerner, PhD

Unfortunately, some people make the following mistake: they try to find explanations for the outrageous actions of others, even if they have no rational basis. So, avoid justifying others and focusing on what you could have done differently to steer clear of becoming a victim.

When we want to identify the motives for a person's actions, we often take steps to normalize them.

Also, don't wonder if the abuser is sorry for what they did if you haven't received a sincere apology from them. Instead, focus on yourself rather than trying to read the thoughts of others.

It's also important not to initiate additional contact with the abuser to share your feelings. Most likely, they already know and are not embarrassed by it. No one can force guilt on others or artificially induce a desire to apologize.

Finally, don't minimize the impact of conflict, trauma, or the forced end of a relationship on your life. It's normal to want to appear strong and invulnerable, yet the ability to experience and let go of challenges indicates your inner strength like nothing else.

Not all stories can have a happy ending where the perpetrators realize their mistakes and the victims sincerely forgive them. So, find the courage to talk about your experience, how it changed you, and what it taught you.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Although there are at least two sides to every story, each person can only describe and take care of their own experiences. To do this wisely, choose the right words for your emotions, accept only those apologies that speak to you, and ignore remarks that try to provoke you. Finally, remember to give yourself time to let go of the experience without minimizing it.

Try this

• Watch your favorite movies and focus on the conflict scenes. How do the characters resolve them? Would you like to learn something from them or, on the contrary, condemn their decisions?
• Which of the five "don'ts" in apologies have you encountered in your life? Practice answering such pseudo-apologies with reason.
• Consider what scenarios after big quarrels work for you — forgetting, forgiving, or letting go. If you already experienced any of them, did they help save the relationship?

Read it till the end?
